textauszug: „Unter sachlich klarer Ansicht verstehe ich das erneute Bewusstsein, dass die Architektur die Kunst der Raumumschliessung ist, dass daher auf den Raum in architek tonischer Beziehung, konstrukti v wie dekorativ, der Hauptwert zu legen ist, und dass infolgedessen ein Gebäude nicht in erster Linie Manifestation nach aussen sein darf. Die Kunst des Baumeisters besteht darin, Räume zu schaffen, und nicht darin, Fassaden zu entwerfen. Eine Raumumschliessung wird aus Mauern hergestellt, und daher manifestiert sich der Raum, oder eine Vielzahl von Räumen, nach aussen als ein mehr oder weniger geschlossener Komplex von Mauern. Man strebe bei der Konzeption eines Gebäudes zuerst nach einer harmonischen Massenverteilung, danach erst richte man seine Aufmerksamkeit auf das Detail. ... Da steht, graurot hochgezogen, die grosse Mauerfläche gegen das bunte Strassengewühl , nach oben dunkler, mit kantiger, einfach schöner Kontur aus der Luft geschnitten; ein prächtiger, natürlich durchgearbeiteter, tausenfarbiger , aber stiller Grund, befleckt mit dunklen Flächen, nur wenige mit reichem plastischen Rahmen, vornehme Verziehrung eines im übrigen schlichten Kleides. Ein ernstes Werk, hebt sich aus dem unordentlichen Wirrwarr rundherum heraus.“ Henrick Petrus Berlache , Über Acrhitektur und Stil 1894 – 1928 „Von der klassischen Zeit lernen wir das Prinzip, das in der Barockzeit sich schon als praktisch anwendbar bewies. Jene Zeit lehrt und die Anwendung der grossen axialen Perspektive, die Geräumigkeit, somit die Abgeschlossenheit des Bildes. Und vom Mittelalter lernen wir schliesslich die verschiedenenartigsten Lösungen im romantischen Geiste, weil auch dieser der lokalen Beschaffenheit wegen, im Städteplan berüksichtigt werden muss.“ H.P. Berlage , Über Städtebau 1909 „Wir bewunderten die vornehme, etwas fröstelnde, sehr reservierte Schmalheit der alten Häuser und die gute Architektur der morderne ; (Amsterdam ist die Stadt, in der neue, architektonische Tendenzen sich am sichtbarsten ausgewirkt haben – die einzige Grossstadt ohne abscheuliche Perispherie , die ich kenne.)“ Klaus Mann, Die Sammlung, Amsterdam 1934 „ In welch kraftvoller Schönheit und einfacher Grösse, vollkommen in seinem Charakter entsprechend, könnte ein Block von Massenwohnungen entstehen, mit impressionistisch-kantiger Silhouette und wenigen, einfach variierten Details an den verschiedenen Eingängen. “ H.P. Berlage , Baukunst und Impressionismus H.P. Berlage - Plan Zuid Amsterdam Philosophy of H . P . Berlage & H ow is this reflected in the architectural composition? H.P. Berlage made different designs for the expansion of Amsterdam South from 1905 ti l l 1915. His f irst design of ‘Plan Zuid ’ is referring to the pitoresq English Gardens of the 19 th century, Romanticism and JugendStill . His final design shows the transition to a Modern approach of Urbanism, based on Monumentality, Totality with clear proportions and a harmonious composition. First Design: Pittoresque and Romanticism I nfluenced by Ebezener Howard and English Pitoresk gardens of William Kent with Enscenering of the route referring to Romanticism. Painting Lorrain. Nature and Jugendstill . Proportions and composition referring to nature. Use of ‘Gulde n S nede ’. Similar as William Morris This desi gn was rejected because of its inefficiency and high price . Final Design: Rejecting Historical Building methods and rejecting Eclecticism Modernism and Monumentality Architecture to improve the living conditions and life of the people . The modern idea of exalt ing the people, r ich and poor people together in one neibo rhoud . To create culture for the people by integrating public places into the public. In Plan Zuid creates B erlage a frequency of strict lines in alternation with public squares with monumental Public buildings such as Stadion , Schools and Churches. The asses have a very wide profile. No small alleys. Also anonymity and criminality ( Bijlmer , south east) Amsterdamse S chool Architecture that is not only about the building on it’s own, but about it’s totality, details, interior , furniture and Applied Arts are merges into one building . With Plan Z uid it’s only about one single house but about the totality of the city Block. Also merging of Architecture and sculptures in the building. Creates a curtain symbolism, Human bodies build into the building by brick layering. Merging different kind of housing typologies, single housing, working class and lower class. Architects: JP Oud, Bakema, Marcel Breuer, Rietveld De Stijl Clear lines an d planes. Influenced by German E xpressionism . Perfect symmetry, unbroken and continuously walls focusing on clear and harmonious proportions and measurements Architects: Piet Mondriaan , T heo van D oesburg , Bart van der Lek Sculptures: Andre Volten Painters: Charley Toorop Berlage breaks in a radical way with Eclectisms and the Historical way of designing of the end of the 19 th century. He has visionary ideas for a better way of living that focusses on a better livingcondition for the people. This can be achieved by architecture that refers to the essence of designing, a harmonious design based on simplicity and symmetry in its composition and measurements. Berlages visionair and revolutionair thinking is also done by other Modernists of the beginning of the 20 th century: Rus sian revoluti on Constructivism , Isadora Duncan, Coco Chanel (creating comfortable clothing, strikes with old fashioned clothing) , A rnold S chönberg , I gor S travinsky ( A tonale ritmes .Opera Oedi poes rex ) Metropolis Fritz Lang, Man R ay , First project (1900-1905) Interference Berlage, Aestethic advisor of the government. Instead of squares as accidentals leftovers, they must be central base, with a public building. Similar as Camillo Sitte Der Stadtebau nach seinen kunstlerischen Grundsatzen . He designed two squares Upper-class and working-class. Idea of Plan zuid was not as a enlargement but as a embellishment/beautification. New train station/railway crossing through the plan. Already was an division about routes and water connection. Final design (1914-1917) In his design he realizes transition between city and landscape. ‘’Daardoor ontstond dat eigenaardige karakter, in aanleg monumentaal, in detail schilderachtig’’ ‘’This created that peculiar character, potentially monumental, picturesque in detail’’ His idea about pictural is connected to the picturaal in the monumental paintings. With this pitoresqness he recognizes the picturality of the Dutch cities, also recognizes the missing to great a big project. Monumentality was lost, because of the lack of religion, he saw the new ideal as the equality of people, when this was achieved a new stijl could me established. Because of the pitoresqness of the dutch cities the waters, the bourgouisie , of the city, design of small bridges the high potention of architectural- development is missing He uses the work of violet l duc to do research to create order and H.P . Berlage Plan Zuid Amsterdam What was the Design philosophy of H.P . Berlage during the beginning of the 20th century and how is this reflected in the architectural composition of Plan Zuid ? H.P . Berlage made different designs for the expansion of Amsterdam South from 1905 till 1915. His first design of ‘Plan Zuid’ is referring to the pitoresq English Gardens of the 19th century, Romanticism and JugendStill. His final design shows the transition to a Modern approach of Urbanism, based on monumentality and totality with clear proportions and a harmonious composition. First Design: 1. Pittoresque and Romanticism Influenced by Ebezener Howard and English Pitoresk gardens of William Kent with Enscenering of the route referring to Romanticism. Painting Lorrain. 2. Nature and Jugendstill. Proportions and composition referring to nature. Use of ‘Gulden Snede’. Similar as William Morris This design was rejected because of its inefficiency and high price. H.P . Berlage Plan Zuid Amsterdam H.P
